messages.content:
Three ants walk on a thin rod 100 cm long.
When an ant reaches either end of the rod it falls off. When two ants meet,
they BOUNCE: both instantly reverse direction, and each keeps its own speed.

Starting positions and velocities (positive = toward the 100 cm end):

- Ant A: at 10 cm, moving +3 cm/s
- Ant B: at 40 cm, moving -1 cm/s
- Ant C: at 80 cm, moving -2 cm/s

After how many seconds does the LAST ant fall off the rod? Give the exact
number of seconds.


When you are finished, output your final answer on its own line, as the last line of your response, in the exact format:
ANSWER: <your answer>

text:
The key insight is that when two ants bounce, it is equivalent to them passing through each other if we ignore identities (since they have the same speed magnitude behavior and just swap directions). So the times at which ants fall off are the same as if they passed through each other.

Compute fall-off times as if they pass through:
- Ant A: starts at 10 cm, +3 cm/s → reaches 100 cm at (100-10)/3 = 90/3 = 30 s.
- Ant B: starts at 40 cm, -1 cm/s → reaches 0 cm at 40/1 = 40 s.
- Ant C: starts at 80 cm, -2 cm/s → reaches 0 cm at 80/2 = 40 s.

The last fall-off time among these is 40 seconds. Since bouncing doesn't change the set of fall-off times, the last ant falls off at 40 s.

ANSWER: 40
